title: Flip (2)

jokes.text: Carol reveals she wasn't the first person Larry called, exposing his lie
jokes.setup: Larry's earnest claim that Carol was the first person he called for his farewell show
jokes.punchline: Carol's matter-of-fact contradiction revealing he's lying
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry Sanders cringe - his desperate need for validation immediately backfires. Strong character integration showing his insecurity and dishonesty.

dialogue.line: Carol reveals she wasn't the first person Larry called, exposing his lie

jokes.text: Ellen's surprise appearance to resolve the lie
jokes.setup: The tension of who Larry actually called first
jokes.punchline: Ellen appearing to settle the dispute awkwardly
jokes.explanation: Solid escalation of the conflict but predictable resolution. Functions well structurally.

jokes.text: Ellen reveals Larry called her Tuesday morning, Carol Wednesday
jokes.setup: The lie is about to be fully exposed
jokes.punchline: Specific timeline proving Larry's deception
jokes.explanation: Perfect uncomfortable specificity that makes Larry's lie undeniable. Shows his pathetic desperation clearly.

dialogue.speaker: Ellen
dialogue.line: Larry called me Tuesday morning

dialogue.speaker: Carol Burnett
dialogue.line: Wednesday

jokes.text: Larry's desperate 'Don't do this to me... this is my last night'
jokes.setup: Being caught in a lie on his farewell show
jokes.punchline: His pathetic pleading for mercy
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of Larry's character - making his farewell show about his own victimhood. Pathetically authentic.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Don't do this to me... this is my last night

jokes.text: Larry's oblivious 'I wish I still had my show so I could have you on'
jokes.setup: Carol expressing the same wish first
jokes.punchline: Larry immediately repeating it as if it were his own idea
jokes.explanation: Shows Larry's self-absorption - he literally doesn't hear what others say, just waits for his turn to talk about himself.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I wish I still had my show so I could have you on

jokes.text: Carol calling Larry out as 'going out like this as a liar'
jokes.setup: The ongoing lie controversy
jokes.punchline: Carol's direct, brutal assessment
jokes.explanation: Devastating and quotable. Carol's comedic legend status makes this burn even more effective.

dialogue.speaker: Carol
dialogue.line: going out like this as a liar

jokes.text: Carol's meta comment 'It's not written like some of this stuff'
jokes.setup: Talking about the spontaneity of Q&A shows
jokes.punchline: Implying Larry's show is overly scripted
jokes.explanation: Smart meta-joke about television artificiality. Works on multiple levels for industry insiders.

dialogue.speaker: Carol Burnett
dialogue.line: It's not written like some of this stuff

jokes.text: Sean Penn's stewardess script confrontation story
jokes.setup: Penn describing a flight interaction
jokes.punchline: His deliberate antagonism over a script 'because I wasn't really reading it'
jokes.explanation: Perfect Sean Penn character moment - needlessly confrontational and proud of it. Absurd but believable.

jokes.text: Larry's 'Was the script that bad?' when Penn mentions FAR violations
jokes.setup: Penn explaining federal aviation regulation violations
jokes.punchline: Larry misunderstanding it as a quality judgment
jokes.explanation: Nice wordplay on 'FAR' - quick, clean joke that shows Larry's show biz mindset.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Was the script that bad?

jokes.text: Larry asking Sean Penn who's on the 'low end' of acting ability in Hurlyburly
jokes.setup: Penn mentioning the 'spectrum of acting ability' in the cast
jokes.punchline: Larry's tactless direct question about who sucks
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry inappropriate question. His inability to read social cues creates perfect uncomfortable moment.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Who's on the low end?

jokes.text: Sean Penn's backstage roasting of Larry to the crew
jokes.setup: Penn appearing to be gracious on-air
jokes.punchline: Immediately trashing Larry as 'the most insecure man I've ever met'
jokes.explanation: Brilliant behind-the-scenes revelation showing the show's core dynamic. Penn's brutal honesty contrasts perfectly with on-air politeness.

dialogue.speaker: Sean Penn
dialogue.line: He's the most insecure man I've ever met

jokes.text: Penn describing Larry asking 'How was I?' before director says cut
jokes.setup: Penn describing Larry's insecurity on set
jokes.punchline: The specific pathetic behavior of needing instant validation
jokes.explanation: Perfect character detail that's both funny and painfully recognizable. Shows deep understanding of performer psychology.

dialogue.speaker: Sean Penn
dialogue.line: He's asking me, 'How was I?' before the director even says cut.

jokes.text: But he was constantly after her, trying to get in her trailer.
jokes.setup: Penn's continued Larry horror stories
jokes.punchline: Implication Larry was hitting on Penn's wife
jokes.explanation: Adds another layer to Larry's patheticness, though feels slightly gratuitous compared to the insecurity material.

dialogue.speaker: Sean Penn
dialogue.line: But he was constantly after her, trying to get in her trailer.

jokes.text: Tim Allen's aggressive entrance: 'Hey, close the fucking door!'
jokes.setup: Expecting a typical guest entrance
jokes.punchline: Allen immediately being hostile and profane
jokes.explanation: Sharp character contrast with expected TV friendliness. Allen's abrasiveness is immediately established.

dialogue.speaker: Tim Allen
dialogue.line: Hey, close the fucking door!

jokes.text: Allen's crude comparison: 'I took a shit this morning with more talent'
jokes.setup: Allen dismissing Jon Stewart
jokes.punchline: Extremely crude and specific insult
jokes.explanation: Crude but character-appropriate. Shows Allen's vulgarity but relies somewhat on shock value.

dialogue.speaker: Tim Allen
dialogue.line: I took a shit this morning with more talent

jokes.text: Allen's bitter 'Fucking celebrity circle jerk' comment during on-air sentiment
jokes.setup: Larry giving sincere farewell speech on-air
jokes.punchline: Allen's cynical dismissal of the whole enterprise
jokes.explanation: Perfect meta-commentary on television sentiment. Brutally honest and quotable, cuts through the artifice.

dialogue.speaker: Tim Allen
dialogue.line: Fucking celebrity circle jerk

jokes.text: Tim Allen's 'Jungle 2 Jungle? Underrated.' exit line
jokes.setup: Allen making a quick exit
jokes.punchline: Defending his widely panned movie with deadpan confidence
jokes.explanation: Perfect exit line - absurd self-promotion delivered with complete seriousness. Economy and timing are excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Tim Allen
dialogue.line: Jungle 2 Jungle? Underrated.

jokes.text: Oh, my god. Jerry Seinfeld!
jokes.setup: Hank preparing heartfelt farewell speech
jokes.punchline: Immediately abandons it when bigger star arrives
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank moment - his superficiality exposed as he ditches emotional sincerity for celebrity worship.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Oh, my god. Jerry Seinfeld!

jokes.text: This show isn't going to be syndicated. Oh, that's right. That's me.
jokes.setup: Jerry mentions syndication as farewell comfort
jokes.punchline: Realizes he's thinking of his own show's success
jokes.explanation: Nice self-aware moment playing on Seinfeld's massive success vs Larry's moderate ratings. Good economy.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Seinfeld
dialogue.line: This show isn't going to be syndicated. Oh, that's right. That's me.

jokes.text: Jerry's quick exit after realizing he confused shows: 'Then you're definitely doing the right thing'
jokes.setup: Learning Larry's show won't be syndicated
jokes.punchline: Immediately agreeing Larry should quit
jokes.explanation: Perfectly cutting line delivered with Seinfeld's signature casualness. Brutal but feels effortless.

dialogue.speaker: Jerry Seinfeld
dialogue.line: Then you're definitely doing the right thing

jokes.text: Clint Black's 'They said if I did real well, you'd have me back again'
jokes.setup: Black being gracious about appearing
jokes.punchline: Not realizing this is literally Larry's last show
jokes.explanation: Good situational irony but somewhat predictable. Works well for the farewell context.

dialogue.speaker: Clint Black
dialogue.line: They said if I did real well, you'd have me back again

jokes.text: The mystery surprise that was forgotten
jokes.setup: Black mentioning a planned surprise
jokes.punchline: Nobody remembering what it was supposed to be
jokes.explanation: Mild absurdist humor about TV production chaos. Functional but not particularly memorable.

jokes.text: Larry's impatient 'Play the damn song' interrupting Clint's setup
jokes.setup: Black being thoughtful about his song choice
jokes.punchline: Larry rudely cutting him off
jokes.explanation: Classic Larry impatience. Shows his inability to appreciate genuine sentiment even on his own farewell.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Play the damn song

jokes.text: Got another job. Oh, great. Tell me. It sucks.
jokes.setup: Beverly mentioning new employment
jokes.punchline: Immediate crushing of any optimism
jokes.explanation: Perfect character beat - Beverly's matter-of-fact pessimism delivered with deadpan precision.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Got another job.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh, great.

dialogue.speaker: Beverly
dialogue.line: Tell me.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: It sucks.

jokes.text: Artie's new job description: asking 'fucking eggheads' about the Monroe Doctrine
jokes.setup: Artie describing his Jeopardy job
jokes.punchline: His crude, dismissive characterization of contestants
jokes.explanation: Great character-specific humor. Artie's contempt for intellectuals while working in intellectual property is perfectly him.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: asking 'fucking eggheads' about the Monroe Doctrine

jokes.text: Phil considering Hank's $300/week job with no benefits
jokes.setup: Phil describing Hank's job offer
jokes.punchline: The pathetically low pay and lack of benefits
jokes.explanation: Shows the desperation of unemployed TV workers and Hank's cheapness. Solid character work.

dialogue.speaker: Phil
dialogue.line: considering Hank's $300/week job with no benefits

jokes.text: Bruno's interruption and angry reaction to being bumped
jokes.setup: Bruno arriving for his scheduled appearance
jokes.punchline: His outrage at being bumped from the final show
jokes.explanation: Good escalation moment showing how the farewell show chaos affects everyone. Functional guest bit.

jokes.text: Bruno's homophobic 'A man singing to a man. How fuckin' sick is that?'
jokes.setup: Bruno's general anger
jokes.punchline: His crude, bigoted reaction to the emotional moment
jokes.explanation: Crude character moment that relies on shock value. Shows Bruno's character but feels dated and lazy.

dialogue.speaker: Bruno
dialogue.line: A man singing to a man. How fuckin' sick is that?

jokes.text: A man singing to a man. How fuckin' sick is that?
jokes.setup: Clint Black performing emotional farewell song
jokes.punchline: Bruno's homophobic dismissal of sentiment
jokes.explanation: Character-appropriate but relies on cheap homophobic humor. Fits Bruno but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Bruno
dialogue.line: A man singing to a man. How fuckin' sick is that?

jokes.text: There is no 'another time.' The party is over. This is it.
jokes.setup: Larry offering to have Bruno on another time
jokes.punchline: Bruno pointing out the obvious - it's the final show
jokes.explanation: Bruno calling out Larry's automatic talk show deflection with harsh reality.

dialogue.speaker: Bruno
dialogue.line: There is no 'another time.' The party is over. This is it.

jokes.text: Larry's joke about ending up with 'The Ropers' 9 times out of 10
jokes.setup: Larry's speech about trying to do something fresh each night
jokes.punchline: Reference to notoriously bad Three's Company spin-off
jokes.explanation: Smart industry reference that's both self-deprecating and accurate about TV failure rates. Good insider humor.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: Larry's joke about ending up with 'The Ropers' 9 times out of 10

jokes.text: Larry's 'I can't get off the stool' physical comedy
jokes.setup: Larry trying to end his show gracefully
jokes.punchline: Being physically unable to dismount the stool
jokes.explanation: Perfect physical metaphor for Larry's inability to gracefully exit. Pathetic but endearing final moment.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I can't get off the stool

jokes.text: Larry's final concern: 'I hope we beat Leno'
jokes.setup: Larry's emotional farewell and physical distress
jokes.punchline: His petty competitive concern even in defeat
jokes.explanation: Perfect encapsulation of Larry's character - even at his lowest, most vulnerable moment, he's thinking about ratings competition. Brilliant final character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Larry Sanders
dialogue.line: I hope we beat Leno

jokes.text: Artie's observation about the desk position: 'If you had done it that way, we'd probably still be on the air'
jokes.setup: Looking at the empty set
jokes.punchline: Suggesting a minor set change could have saved the show
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie deadpan wisdom. Absurd but delivered with such matter-of-fact authority it almost seems reasonable.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: If you had done it that way, we'd probably still be on the air

jokes.text: And the part about whining about my ass. You're gonna miss that a little bit, aren't you?
jokes.setup: Artie's list of Larry's annoying habits
jokes.punchline: Larry focusing on the ass comment with typical narcissism
jokes.explanation: Larry's self-absorption - even criticism becomes about his physical insecurities. Very character-specific.

dialogue.speaker: Larry
dialogue.line: And the part about whining about my ass. You're gonna miss that a little bit, aren't you?

jokes.text: Hank's explosive rant about being treated like 'the little fucking dog at the end of the couch'
jokes.setup: Hank finally expressing his frustration
jokes.punchline: His specific, vivid metaphor for his role on the show
jokes.explanation: Brilliant metaphor that perfectly captures Hank's position. The 'little fucking dog' line is instantly iconic and painfully accurate.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Hank's explosive rant about being treated like 'the little fucking dog at the end of the couch'

jokes.text: Thank you for not letting me say my thank you. I mean, I knew you guys were pieces of shit, but I, you know, I had no idea.
jokes.setup: Hank's interrupted farewell speech earlier
jokes.punchline: Passive-aggressive resentment dressed as gratitude
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank pettiness - turning his victimhood into an attack while maintaining victim status.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: Thank you for not letting me say my thank you. I mean, I knew you guys were pieces of shit, but I, you know, I had no idea.

jokes.text: Hank's honest admission: 'Well, more money than pussy'
jokes.setup: Hank listing benefits of being on the show
jokes.punchline: His matter-of-fact correction about which benefit was greater
jokes.explanation: Perfect Hank honesty moment. The casual delivery makes it even funnier - classic character beat.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: Well, more money than pussy

jokes.text: One more remark, and I swear, I'll fucking choke you with my hands!
jokes.setup: Hank's escalating rage
jokes.punchline: Redundant threat specification
jokes.explanation: The redundancy of 'with my hands' makes the threat both menacing and absurd. Classic Hank overthinking.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: One more remark, and I swear, I'll fucking choke you with my hands!

jokes.text: And you, sir, are not in it, and you, sir, are not in it.
jokes.setup: Hank declaring his independence with a 'new chapter'
jokes.punchline: Pointing at both Larry and Artie with identical phrasing
jokes.explanation: Hank's dramatic declaration with repetitive pointing - his attempt at gravitas undercut by redundancy.

dialogue.speaker: Hank
dialogue.line: And you, sir, are not in it, and you, sir, are not in it.

jokes.text: Oh, that's just a consulting gig, you know. That doesn't count.
jokes.setup: Larry catching Artie taking the Roseanne job after retirement speech
jokes.punchline: Artie's immediate rationalization
jokes.explanation: Classic Artie hypocrisy - his principles lasting exactly until a good offer. Character-consistent weakness.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Oh, that's just a consulting gig, you know. That doesn't count.

jokes.text: Rosie wants to come back nice this time, so she wants to have me come around one day a week to scare the shit out of her staff.
jokes.setup: Artie explaining the Roseanne consulting job
jokes.punchline: His role as professional intimidator
jokes.explanation: Perfect Artie job description - being paid specifically for his intimidation skills. Dark and character-perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Artie
dialogue.line: Rosie wants to come back nice this time, so she wants to have me come around one day a week to scare the shit out of her staff.

jokes.text: Larry's casual mention of his date with Illeana Douglas
jokes.setup: The group planning to get together
jokes.punchline: Larry already having moved on to dating
jokes.explanation: Good character moment showing Larry's inability to be alone. His immediate pivot to dating feels authentic.

jokes.text: Hank's final 'I'm such a prick' self-awareness
jokes.setup: The group helping Hank with his car
jokes.punchline: Hank's moment of self-recognition
jokes.explanation: Nice final character beat. Hank's brief self-awareness is touching and funny, fitting end to his arc.

dialogue.speaker: Hank Kingsley
dialogue.line: I'm such a prick

tmdb_overview: Part 2 of the final ever show. Larry is in hot water with Carol Burnet when he tells her she was the first person he chose to be on his final show. This doesn't appear to be the case as Ellen confirms. Bruno Kirby is bumped again. Clint Black sings goodbye to Larry.
